local_names.romanized_local: Bīng níngméng chá / Dung3 ning4 mung4 caa4

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301099). No specific class exists for iced lemon tea or packaged sweetened lemon tea. Closest parent match used; exact class unavailable.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific facet code for sweetened packaged iced lemon tea exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for packaged iced lemon tea. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food product codes at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 ml (one bottle or can), which is the typical retail unit size for packaged iced lemon tea sold in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional analysis of beverages in local coffee shops — Singapore Health Promotion Board
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore provides nutritional data for iced lemon tea as served in local coffee shops and kopitiams. The packaged product variant is closely aligned with the HPB reference values; minor micronutrient values (potassium, magnesium, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Tea, ready-to-drink, lemon flavored' (NDB No. 14355) where HPB does not report those fields. Saturated fat is reported as 0.0 g consistent with a tea-based beverage containing no dairy or fat-bearing ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged iced lemon tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated by analogy with sweetened ready-to-drink tea beverages reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The primary carbohydrate is refined sucrose (~8.5 g/100 g), which drives a moderate glycemic response. Lemon acidity and black tea tannins may modestly attenuate the glycemic peak relative to plain sugar-sweetened beverages. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 31.1) / 100 ≈ 20.2 (based on 350 g serving × 8.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 30.8 g carbohydrate, rounded to 31.1 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (5 mg per 100 g); however, caffeine and high sugar intake are secondary metabolic concerns.
hypertension.guidance: Packaged iced lemon tea is low in sodium and does not directly raise blood pressure through salt load, but individuals with hypertension should be aware that caffeine can cause transient blood pressure elevation and that chronic high sugar intake contributes to metabolic syndrome, which worsens hypertensive outcomes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar content (~8.5 g per 100 g; ~29.8 g per 350 ml serving) with a medium glycemic index of 65 and a glycemic load of approximately 20 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit packaged iced lemon tea due to its high added sugar content and moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; sugar-free or reduced-sugar variants should be chosen where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and sodium, but high added sugar intake is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular risk with regular consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While packaged iced lemon tea contains no saturated fat or significant sodium, its high sugar content (approximately 30 g per 350 ml serving) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and adverse lipid profiles with habitual consumption, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional use or choose unsweetened alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged Iced Lemon Tea is a commercially produced, ready-to-drink (RTD) sweetened black tea beverage flavoured with lemon juice or lemon flavouring, sold in bottles or cans typically in 350 ml to 500 ml serving sizes. It is widely consumed across Singapore, Hong Kong, and Malaysia and is a staple of kopitiams (traditional coffee shops) and cha chaan tengs (Hong Kong-style cafes), where a freshly prepared version is also popular. The packaged variant is made by steeping black tea, adding sugar or high-fructose corn syrup, incorporating lemon juice or citric acid and lemon flavouring, and pasteurising the product before sealing. The flavour profile is sweet and mildly tart, with the astringency of black tea tannins balanced by the citrus acidity of lemon and the sweetness of added sugar. The texture is thin and watery, similar to other RTD tea beverages. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 35 kcal, 8.8 g of carbohydrate (of which 8.5 g is sugar), 0.1 g of protein, and negligible fat. It contains a modest amount of vitamin C (5.5 mg per 100 g) from the lemon component and trace amounts of potassium, calcium, and B vitamins from the tea base. The beverage has a medium glycemic index of approximately 65 and delivers a glycemic load of around 20 per standard 350 ml serving, making it a significant source of rapidly absorbed sugar. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or those on calorie-restricted diets without modification. It is low in sodium and fat, making it less concerning for hypertension and cardiovascular disease from a salt or lipid perspective, though its high sugar content remains a metabolic risk factor. The caffeine content from black tea provides a mild stimulant effect. Common Singaporean and Malaysian ordering customisations include 'siew dai' (less sugar) and 'kosong' (no sugar) variants.

Iced lemon tea calories per bottle
Is packaged iced lemon tea suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and sugar values are sourced from the Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB), an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. Micronutrient values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for RTD lemon tea, also an approved source. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened RTD tea, which is the approved GI source. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a sweetened packaged tea beverage.
notes: fat_g is 0.0: this is a tea-based beverage containing no fat-bearing ingredients (no dairy, no oil); fat is naturally zero and confirmed zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for RTD lemon tea. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all 0.0 for the same reason. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: tea and lemon juice contain negligible provitamin A; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: vitamin B12 is not present in plant-derived beverages; naturally zero. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: no dietary vitamin D source in this beverage; naturally zero. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. water_g is set to -1.0 as precise moisture content for the packaged product is not reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry used; estimated water content would be approximately 90 g per 100 g based on the energy and macronutrient profile but is not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not reported. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (65 × 30.8) / 100 ≈ 20.0, rounded to 20.2 using 31.1 g carbohydrate (350 g × 8.88 g/100 g). GI is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this specific packaged product was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 class exists for packaged sweetened iced lemon tea. This record covers the generic packaged/RTD category; nutritional values may vary by brand.
